theory.canonical_statement: frac\{16\}\{15\},quad frac\{10\}\{9\},quad text\{and\}quad chi := frac\{32\}\{27\}. / ] Individually, these ratios represent small algebraic deviations from exact closure. Taken together, they form a structured ladder that signals the onset of qualitative regime change when embedded in a geometrically curved or topologically nontrivial system. The role of /pi/ in this process is not to define the ratios themselves, but to convert their algebraic excess into a physically meaningful phase surplus. paragraph \{Definition (Algebraic Surplus).\} For any multiplicative step Ss>0S, define the algebraic surplus begin\{equation\} delta(s) : =s-1 . end\{equation\} In particular, begin\{equation\} delta/!left(frac\{16\}\{15\}right)=frac\{1\}\{15\},quad delta/!left(frac\{10\}\{9\}right)=frac\{1\}\{9\},quad delta(chi)=frac\{5\}\{27\}. end\{equation\} paragraph \{Definition (/pi/-Weighted Phase Surplus).\} Define the /pi/-weighted phase surplus begin\{equation\} Deltaphi(s) := pi/,delta(s) = pi(s-1), end\{equation\} which has the interpretation of an angular excess measured in radians. For the ratios of interest, begin\{align\} Deltaphi/!left(frac\{16\}\{15\}right) \&= frac\{pi\}\{15\} approximately 12\textasciicircum{}circ, Deltaphi/!left(frac\{10\}\{9\}right) \&= frac\{pi\}\{9\} approximately 20\textasciicircum{}circ, Deltaphi(chi) \&= frac\{5pi\}\{27\} approximately 33.34\textasciicircum{}circ. end\{align\} paragraph\{Interpretation.\} The quantities above define a monotone angular ladder. Each step is small in purely algebraic terms, yet becomes geometrically significant once weighted by /pi/. This distinction reflects the difference between rational mode-locking and curvature-induced phase drift. paragraph \{Definition (Critical Phase Margin).\} Let /phi\_c in (0,pi)/ denote a system-dependent critical phase margin, representing the maximum surplus compatible with topological closure on a single branch. paragraph\{Theorem ( /pi/-Weighted Surplus Criterion ).\} begin\{equation\} label\{eq:pi\_surplus\_criterion\} boxed\{ pi(s-1) >= phi\_c /;/;Longrightarrow/;/; text\{topological regime change\} \} end\{equation\} paragraph \{Canonical Choice.\} For lemniscatic manifolds and bifurcation problems involving branch exchange, a natural reference scale is begin\{equation\} phi\_c := frac\{pi\}\{6\}. end\{equation\} Under this choice, begin\{equation\} frac\{Deltaphi(chi)\}\{phi\_c\} = frac\{(5pi/27)\}\{(pi/6)\} = frac\{10\}\{9\}. end\{equation\} Thus the /chi/-step exceeds the critical margin by a factor of exactly /10/9/, establishing a closed algebraic relation between the three principal ratios /[ frac\{16\}\{15\}, quad frac\{10\}\{9\}, quad frac\{32\}\{27\}.
theory.technical_interpretation: frac\{16\}\{15\},quad frac\{10\}\{9\},quad text\{and\}quad chi := frac\{32\}\{27\}. / ] Individually, these ratios represent small algebraic deviations from exact closure. Taken together, they form a structured ladder that signals the onset of qualitative regime change when embedded in a geometrically curved or topologically nontrivial system. The role of /pi/ in this process is not to define the ratios themselves, but to convert their algebraic excess into a physically meaningful phase surplus. paragraph \{Definition (Algebraic Surplus).\} For any multiplicative step Ss>0S, define the algebraic surplus begin\{equation\} delta(s) : =s-1 . end\{equation\} In particular, begin\{equation\} delta/!left(frac\{16\}\{15\}right)=frac\{1\}\{15\},quad delta/!left(frac\{10\}\{9\}right)=frac\{1\}\{9\},quad delta(chi)=frac\{5\}\{27\}. end\{equation\} paragraph \{Definition (/pi/-Weighted Phase Surplus).\} Define the /pi/-weighted phase surplus begin\{equation\} Deltaphi(s) := pi/,delta(s) = pi(s-1), end\{equation\} which has the interpretation of an angular excess measured in radians. For the ratios of interest, begin\{align\} Deltaphi/!left(frac\{16\}\{15\}right) \&= frac\{pi\}\{15\} approximately 12\textasciicircum{}circ, Deltaphi/!left(frac\{10\}\{9\}right) \&= frac\{pi\}\{9\} approximately 20\textasciicircum{}circ, Deltaphi(chi) \&= frac\{5pi\}\{27\} approximately 33.34\textasciicircum{}circ. end\{align\} paragraph\{Interpretation.\} The quantities above define a monotone angular ladder. Each step is small in purely algebraic terms, yet becomes geometrically significant once weighted by /pi/. This distinction reflects the difference between rational mode-locking and curvature-induced phase drift. paragraph \{Definition (Critical Phase Margin).\} Let /phi\_c in (0,pi)/ denote a system-dependent critical phase margin, representing the maximum surplus compatible with topological closure on a single branch. paragraph\{Theorem ( /pi/-Weighted Surplus Criterion ).\} begin\{equation\} label\{eq:pi\_surplus\_criterion\} boxed\{ pi(s-1) >= phi\_c /;/;Longrightarrow/;/; text\{topological regime change\} \} end\{equation\} paragraph \{Canonical Choice.\} For lemniscatic manifolds and bifurcation problems involving branch exchange, a natural reference scale is begin\{equation\} phi\_c := frac\{pi\}\{6\}. end\{equation\} Under this choice, begin\{equation\} frac\{Deltaphi(chi)\}\{phi\_c\} = frac\{(5pi/27)\}\{(pi/6)\} = frac\{10\}\{9\}. end\{equation\} Thus the /chi/-step exceeds the critical margin by a factor of exactly /10/9/, establishing a closed algebraic relation between the three principal ratios /[ frac\{16\}\{15\}, quad frac\{10\}\{9\}, quad frac\{32\}\{27\}.
